policy_statement: Create infiltration gardens and other aquifer recharge solutions (e.g., trenches and infiltration channels) as part of soil conservation and fertility improvement.
mechanism_description: Creating infiltration gardens, trenches, and infiltration channels increases infiltration to recharge aquifers and improve local water availability and resilience during dry periods.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (drought/variable rainfall) → ↑ infiltration & groundwater recharge → ↑ water storage/availability → ↓ exposure/sensitivity to water scarcity
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system: Implement green/gray recharge infrastructure (infiltration gardens, trenches, infiltration channels) and associated land/soil management to route stormwater or surface flows into the ground.  
(2) Mediating process: Enhanced infiltration increases percolation below the root zone, replenishing aquifers and raising/maintaining groundwater levels; this buffers interannual rainfall variability by storing water subsurface.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Greater water security and operational resilience during drought and dry spells (reduced likelihood/severity of water shortages and restrictions), improving adaptive capacity of communities and ecosystems.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved drought resilience via increased groundwater recharge and more reliable water availability during dry periods.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism acts on hydrology (infiltration → aquifer recharge) to buffer drought impacts and improve water availability—an adaptation outcome.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While recharge features can yield Resource Efficiency (e.g., reduced imported water) or Mitigation (e.g., less pumping energy) co-benefits, those depend on local water/energy systems and are not the guaranteed primary effect of aquifer recharge infrastructure.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy’s stated goal is primarily emissions reduction via reduced pumping/energy, it could be framed as mitigation/resource efficiency at the policy level; however, the canonical mechanism here is explicitly “water resilience,” so the dominant effect remains adaptation.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy implementing infiltration-based aquifer recharge is fundamentally aimed at increasing subsurface water storage and reliability under drought/variability, which is adaptation-relevant across contexts.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any secondary classification (e.g., mitigation co-benefits, ecosystem co-benefits) should be assigned based on policy-specific evidence and quantified intent, not assumed from the mechanism alone.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential co-benefits include reduced energy use/emissions from groundwater pumping or desalination/imported water, improved urban cooling/greening, and flood peak reduction; however, these are context-dependent and do not supersede the primary drought-resilience pathway.
key_indicators: "Creation of infiltration gardens"; "aquifer recharge solutions"; "trenches"; "infiltration channels"
co_benefits: Reduced flooding/runoff impacts; improved soil moisture and fertility; water quality improvement via filtration; urban cooling/greening and biodiversity benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Could alternatively be framed as a planning/strategy or regulatory requirement if the source referenced standards/permits; here it is phrased as implementation/creation, so infrastructure investment is most fitting.
